summary.overview: Indiana is experiencing an acute energy affordability crisis, with average residential electric bills surging approximately 17.5% in the 12-month period ending July 2025—the largest single-year jump in two decades—driven by a wave of rate increases from all five major investor-owned utilities. In response, Governor Mike Braun signed landmark legislation (HEA 1002) in February 2026 introducing performance-based ratemaking, low-income assistance requirements, and summer disconnection protections, while the newly reconstituted IURC under Chair Andy Zay launched a statewide Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability in March 2026, holding public hearings and a listening tour across ten Indiana communities. Compounding these pressures are a federal DOE emergency order forcing Indiana coal plants to remain open past their planned 2025 retirement dates, an IURC investigation into NIPSCO overcharging 3,500 gas customers double their actual usage, a pending AES Indiana rate case with a IURC final order deadline of June 24, 2026, and federal threats to eliminate LIHEAP funding—leaving more than 100,000 low-income Hoosier households who rely on the Energy Assistance Program in a precarious position.

messaging_recommendations.audience: Low-income ratepayers and households receiving energy assistance
messaging_recommendations.message: You are not alone—Indiana's energy affordability crisis is a structural problem, not a personal failure. Know your rights under HEA 1002, including new protections against disconnection on dangerously hot days and your right to enroll in a low-income assistance program that every investor-owned utility must now offer.

messaging_recommendations.audience: Indiana state legislators and IURC commissioners
messaging_recommendations.message: The record number of customer complaints in 2026, the NIPSCO overbilling scandal, and the 17.5% single-year bill spike show that existing regulatory tools are insufficient; use the Investigative Inquiry findings to mandate tracker reform, strengthen performance-based ratemaking benchmarks, and protect LIHEAP-eligible households from compounding rate hikes.

Indiana households are paying the highest electric bills in the region at a time when federal energy assistance is under existential threat; transparent, plain-language billing and enforceable affordability standards—not voluntary commitments—are what Hoosier families need and what HEA 1002 has begun to require.

The new performance-based ratemaking framework in HEA 1002 ties your return on equity to customer affordability outcomes and service restoration metrics, creating a direct financial incentive to lower bills and improve reliability rather than simply maximizing rate base growth through successive rate cases.

rate_cases.description: Duke Energy Indiana filed for a two-phase electric base rate increase of approximately $491.5 million (16.2%). The IURC approved a significantly reduced increase of approximately $295.7 million in annual revenues, implemented in steps beginning March 2025.
rate_cases.rate_change: +$295.7 million annually (~$19.16/month for typical residential customer using 1,000 kWh)

rate_cases.description: Duke Energy sought to recover approximately $88 million from ratepayers for coal ash compliance costs through an environmental cost recovery mechanism. The Indiana Court of Appeals ruled in August 2025 that the IURC 'impermissibly applied' Indiana law retroactively in approving the recovery.
rate_cases.rate_change: ~$88 million recovery reversed by Court of Appeals

rate_cases.description: AES Indiana filed for a $192.9 million base rate increase in June 2025 (13.5% phased over two steps: ~7.5% in Q2 2026 and ~6% in January 2027). A partial settlement of approximately $90 million was reached with industrial customers and the City of Indianapolis in October 2025, but the OUCC and Citizens Action Coalition opposed the settlement; the case is fully briefed with a final IURC order deadline of June 24, 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: Requested +$192.9M; settlement at ~$90M increase; OUCC recommended a rate reduction of $21.2M

rate_cases.description: NIPSCO filed for an electric base rate increase of approximately $368.7 million. A settlement agreement was reached reducing the increase to approximately $257 million; the IURC approved the settlement on June 26, 2025, with rates phased in beginning July 2025 through early 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: +$257 million annually (~$23/month or 16.75% for average residential customer using 672 kWh)

rate_cases.description: The IURC opened a formal investigation into NIPSCO in November 2025 after the utility discovered and belatedly disclosed that a rollout of new automated meter reading technology resulted in 3,542 natural gas customers being charged double their actual usage. Newly unsealed records in March 2026 showed NIPSCO discovered the problem in October 2024 but did not inform the IURC until November 2025.
rate_cases.rate_change: NIPSCO credited approximately $1.4 million to overcharged accounts; investigation ongoing

rate_cases.description: CenterPoint Energy filed for a three-phase electric rate increase of $118.8 million (16.02%). The IURC approved an $80 million increase on February 3, 2025, with final phase taking effect in early 2026; the OUCC found an increase of $48.3 million was warranted.
rate_cases.rate_change: +$80 million annually (~$35/month for average residential customer using 799 kWh, phased)

rate_cases.utility: NIPSCO (Northern Indiana Public Service Company) — GenCo
rate_cases.description: NIPSCO received IURC approval to create a subsidiary (NIPSCO Generation/GenCo) intended to structurally isolate large-load (data center) generation costs from residential and small commercial ratepayers, allowing data center customers to be served under separate contracts without shifting infrastructure costs to ordinary customers.
rate_cases.rate_change: NIPSCO projects ~$1 billion in ratepayer savings over 15 years from data center cost isolation

articles.headline: Gov. Braun signs House Bill 1002 to tackle Indiana's energy affordability crisis
articles.summary: Governor Mike Braun signed HEA 1002 into law on February 26, 2026, a sweeping energy bill backed with bipartisan support. The law introduces performance-based ratemaking tied to customer affordability metrics, requires utilities to offer low-income assistance programs by July 1, 2026, mandates levelized billing for EAP-enrolled households, and creates a summer disconnection moratorium on days when the heat index is forecast to reach 95°F or higher.

articles.headline: Indiana utility regulators call in 'big five' energy providers for affordability investigation
articles.summary: IURC Chair Andy Zay announced the commission would conduct a formal Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability, calling in all five major investor-owned electric utilities (AES Indiana, CenterPoint Energy, Duke Energy, Indiana Michigan Power, and NIPSCO). Zay said the current balance between utility profits and customer bills feels 'dramatically out of whack' for many Hoosiers, and the inquiry is the beginning of a process to address rising customer outrage.

articles.headline: 'Big five' Indiana utilities defend rates in regulator questioning
articles.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle / Northwest Indiana Business Magazine
articles.summary: At the March 24, 2026 IURC Investigative Inquiry, utility representatives argued that customer usage and extreme temperatures—not just rates—are the primary drivers of high bills. AES Indiana President Brandi Davis-Handy noted her company's bills have largely tracked inflation, while CenterPoint's Indiana president Michael Roeder touted a commitment to stable rates through 2027; the IURC announced it would move to a second phase of inquiry and a statewide listening tour.

articles.headline: NIPSCO overcharged 3,500 natural gas customers, newly unsealed records show
articles.summary: Newly unsealed IURC records revealed NIPSCO discovered in October 2024 that faulty installation of new automated meter reading technology caused 3,542 customers to be billed double their actual gas usage, but the utility did not notify state regulators for nearly a year—until November 2025. NIPSCO credited approximately $1.4 million to affected accounts, covering 50% of gas usage charges over a 12-month period, while 317 customers were billed at half their true usage and will not be required to repay the difference.

articles.headline: Consumer advocates: Spike in electric bills shows Indiana energy plan isn't working
articles.summary: Citizens Action Coalition Program Director Ben Inskeep said 2025 marked the highest year-over-year jump in Indiana electric bills in two decades, with NIPSCO customers seeing the largest increase of nearly $50/month, followed by CenterPoint customers at $44/month. The average Indiana resident was paying $28 more per month for electricity than in the prior year, leading advocates to say the 2023 utility regulatory law is failing consumers.

articles.headline: Indiana appeals court rules that regulators 'impermissibly' let Duke raise rates for coal cleanup
articles.summary: The Indiana Court of Appeals ruled that the IURC impermissibly applied Indiana law retroactively when it allowed Duke Energy to recover approximately $88 million in coal ash compliance costs from ratepayers through a rate increase. The three-judge panel reversed the IURC's order, meaning Duke Energy customers may not have to pay for those coal cleanup costs, in a case brought by Citizens Action Coalition and Earthjustice.

articles.headline: Your electric bill is going to change. New law aims to stabilize utilities
articles.summary: WFYI explained the major consumer-facing impacts of HEA 1002: Indiana residents will see new billing options including automatic levelized billing for low-income households enrolled in energy assistance programs, utilities must submit quarterly customer data reports to the OUCC, and a new disconnection moratorium will protect customers on the hottest summer days when the National Weather Service forecasts a heat index of 95°F or higher within 48 hours.

articles.headline: AES Indiana customers told regulators their stacks of utility bills don't add up
articles.summary: At an IURC listening session focused on AES Indiana customers in Indianapolis, residents brought stacks of utility bills to show regulators their financial strain and confusion over charges. IURC Chair Andy Zay, who attended alongside commissioners Bob Deig and Anthony Swinger, said the commission was investigating because the state has heard from residents about their financial pains and the balance between utility and customer interests must be restored.

articles.headline: Indiana's Energy Assistance Program now open: What you need to know
articles.summary: Indiana's Energy Assistance Program (EAP/LIHEAP) opened for applications on October 1, 2025, accepting submissions through April 20, 2026, with FY 2026 funding totaling $87.1 million for the state. The federally funded program provides a one-time annual benefit paid directly to utility companies on behalf of more than 100,000 low-income Hoosier households, though benefit amounts for 2025-2026 were expected to be lower than prior years.

articles.headline: DOE orders Indiana coal units totaling more than 950 MW to run past retirement dates
articles.summary: U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright issued emergency orders on December 23, 2025, directing NIPSCO and CenterPoint Energy to keep coal-fired generating units at the Schahfer and F.B. Culley stations operational past their planned December 31, 2025 retirement dates, citing Midwest grid stability concerns. Citizens Action Coalition warned the forced operation of the aging, costly coal units would result in higher bills for Hoosiers already reeling from record-high rate increases, and Earthjustice subsequently challenged the orders in federal court.

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Chairman
stakeholders.quote: That balance feels dramatically out of whack right now for many, many Hoosiers and today is the beginning of a process that we are labeling an investigative inquiry. We're asking our five investor-owned utilities that we regulate on a week-in, week-out basis … to come to this room and have an open dialogue.
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle / Daily Journal news conference coverage

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Chairman
stakeholders.quote: Energy affordability is one of the most pressing challenges states are facing today. We're seeing rising energy costs driven by a range of factors. (paraphrased from published interview)

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Indiana Utility Consumer Counselor
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: The settlement agreement filed by AES and other parties shows the utility's initial request was inflated and unnecessary. The OUCC stands by its initial recommendations for a reduction from current rates and will oppose the utility's settlement agreement.
stakeholders.source: OUCC press release / Daily Journal / Indiana Capital Chronicle

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Indiana Utility Consumer Counselor
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: My team is recommending additional reductions that would reduce the utility's current rates and help lessen the burden on ratepayers. We received thousands of consumer comments in this case, highlighting serious hardships many customers are facing.
stakeholders.source: OUCC news release on AES Indiana rate case recommendations

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: For too long, Hoosiers have been footing the bill for decisions they didn't make and policies they didn't ask for. Indiana's regulatory system should be built to protect consumers, but that goal can only be achieved if lawmakers give regulators the tools and authority it needs. The solutions have been ready for years. The need is now incredibly urgent.
stakeholders.source: WBIW / Indiana Conservation Voters policy plan press release

stakeholders.name: Ben Inskeep
stakeholders.title: Program Director
stakeholders.quote: We've lost a lot of trust with NIPSCO. There's a massive outcry right now. The Indiana Utility Regulatory Commission said more than half the complaints it has received so far in 2026 have been about NIPSCO.
stakeholders.source: Latitude Media, 'In Indiana, an anatomy of data center opposition'

stakeholders.name: Ben Inskeep
stakeholders.title: Program Director
stakeholders.quote: 2025 marked the highest year-over-year jump in electric bills in two decades, which shows the law isn't working. For example, NIPSCO customers in northwest Indiana are seeing a nearly $50 a month increase.
stakeholders.source: Indiana Public Radio / WFYI / WBOI coverage of CAC electric bill analysis

stakeholders.name: Alaina Shonkwiler
stakeholders.title: State Representative (R-Noblesville), author of HB 1002
stakeholders.quote: At its core, House Bill 1002 is about balance. It recognizes that Indiana needs strong financial, stable utilities that can invest in the infrastructure, modernize the grid and meet growing demand at the same time.
stakeholders.source: WFYI Public Media, HB 1002 committee hearing coverage

stakeholders.name: Mike Braun
stakeholders.title: Governor
stakeholders.quote: Affordability is why I support Rep. Alaina Shonkwiler's bill to hold investor-owned utility companies accountable to provide affordable energy before they can increase their profit margin. And it's why I appointed three ratepayer-conscious members, now constituting a majority, to the Indiana Utility Regulatory Commission — to make decisions in the public interest.
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle, State of the State speech coverage

stakeholders.name: Jim Pressel
stakeholders.title: State Representative (R-Rolling Prairie)
stakeholders.quote: We are now asking the IURC and NIPSCO to review current utility rates and take more consideration of the customers who are struggling to pay their energy bills. (paraphrased from letter to IURC Chair Andy Zay)
stakeholders.source: 21Alive / WNDU news coverage of letter to IURC

stakeholders.name: Brandi Davis-Handy
stakeholders.title: President
stakeholders.quote: AES Indiana's residential bills have largely tracked inflation over time. (paraphrased from testimony at March 24, 2026 IURC Investigative Inquiry on Energy Affordability)
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle / Northwest Indiana Business Magazine coverage of IURC Inquiry
